1_answer_correctness.justification: Success is fully deterministic and verifiable in code: terminals expose a /status endpoint and the benchmark checks 'online'/'done' flags after running the model-authored main.py against seeded terminal configs. Ground truth (auth tokens, encodings) is generated from seeds and validated by exact match; extensive unit_tests.py per terminal confirm correctness.

2_construct_validity.justification: Claims to measure cross-run learning/progress retention via increasingly complex nested-terminal tasks; implementation operationalizes this with a persistent filesystem across runs, a difficulty-graded sequence of generators (T1 .. T1|T2|T3|T4|T5 chains), and a score that rewards passing harder generators while penalizing iterations spent (efficiency). Design tightly matches the stated capability.

3_code_quality.justification: Large, modular, well-documented codebase (envs, infrastructure, task_generator, telemetry, per-terminal modules with markdown specs and unit/trace tests). Uses kbench @task decorators, isolated VFS per run with sandbox-escape checks, retries, graceful exception handling, and dual GenAI/OpenAI tool loops. The thin notebook itself is sparse but the executed logic is high quality.

4_sample_size.justification: TaskSpecGenerator uses tasks_count=50 per generator and there are 10 generators with seed-derived variation, far exceeding 20 evaluation items; _find_easiest_unsolved_task iterates over the full set of seeded validating terminals.

5_metric_design.justification: Score combines two distinct dimensions: success (number of fully-passed difficulty generators) and efficiency (penalty proportional to total iterations spent), with telemetry also tracking token metrics and terminal-activation steps. The compound success-plus-efficiency score is justified in the system prompt and goes well beyond single accuracy.

6_problem_statement.justification: Notebook markdown only explains setup steps; the capability framing (mastering activation of unknown nested-terminal configs, learning across runs) appears in the system.md prompt rather than a clear task-page problem statement. Capability is nameable but motivation/gap relative to existing evals is not articulated for a reader.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Dataset source code is extensively documented: per-terminal .md specs (e.g., sys32 delta-base32 encoding), docstrings, trace_test/unit_tests, and detailed system prompt describing protocol and scoring. Combined notebook + dataset give a reproducible picture, though the notebook markdown alone is thin.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (token/iteration-efficient skill acquisition and retention across runs) with a built-in difficulty gradient acting as an experimental control: easy single-terminal tasks up to deep composite chains, with score deltas attributable to how far the agent progresses and how efficiently.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly structured into 10 difficulty-graded generators (single terminal, repeated, mixed-type, composite shuffled), and the validation loop reports which generator/task index the agent fails on (failed_on_generator/failed_on_task), enabling a clear performance gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Notebook runs a single injected kbench.llm and the captured execution errored on import (AttributeError: LocalEnvironment). No multi-model results are present in the notebook.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Combines deterministic checks (regex bracket extraction, strict word-count window, lexical guillotine over a forbidden-token list) with an LLM 'senate' of 4 judges scoring 3-4 binary criteria at a 75% pass threshold. The judge rubric covers multiple dimensions but is pass/fail only with no anchored examples or graded scale, so verification is partly subjective.

2_construct_validity.justification: Claims to test 'epistemic exile'/chronological-purity and conceptual mapping; the paired English-baseline vs Ge'ez/Spanish variants give some control. However heavy reliance on rigid formatting (brackets), a strict word-count guillotine, and forbidden-token banning conflates the target with instruction-following and lexical-avoidance compliance, introducing moderate confounds.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional and reasonably structured (extraction, deterministic guards, judge aggregation), but each notebook is a single code cell with no markdown, magic-string criteria, and a hard dependency on exactly 4 specific judge models keyed by name (FATAL ERROR if absent). Minimal error handling beyond early returns.

4_sample_size.justification: Each task notebook issues exactly one llm.prompt over a single hand-crafted scenario; even taken across the five sibling notebooks it is only ~5 items, far below the 10-item threshold. Per-task it is a single evaluation item.

5_metric_design.justification: Multiple checks are computed (word-count, lexical absence, several judge criteria), but they largely measure the same construct (faithful in-period explanation) and reduce to pass/fail. No calibration/latency dimension and no discussion of judge biases (the 4-judge senate mitigates single-judge variance but this is not framed as bias mitigation).

6_problem_statement.justification: Task name and description name the capability (e.g., 'Evaluating Epistemic Exile: Narrow AI vs AGI in Ge'ez') and scenario, but there is no broader research motivation or articulation of the gap relative to existing evaluations.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Notebooks contain zero markdown cells; there is no documentation of dataset provenance, scoring rules, judge selection rationale, or metric interpretation beyond inline expectation strings. A reader must reverse-engineer the code.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a coherent domain (anachronism-constrained conceptual translation/empathy under lexical constraints) and the English-vs-target-language pairing is a partial control, but the single-item-per-notebook design and bundled formatting constraints prevent isolating a specific mechanism from instruction-following.

9_item_diversity.justification: Across the five notebooks there is natural variation (different eras, figures, languages, topics) and an English baseline vs non-English condition, but within any single task there is exactly one item and no structured difficulty tiers or per-condition breakdown reported in code.

10_model_coverage.justification: The target model under test is a single injected kbench.llm; the four 'senate' models serve as judges, not as evaluated models. No multi-model evaluation results are present.

1_answer_correctness.justification: A deterministic World-Swap engine generates ground-truth correct and naive answers (operator/physics/linguistic/logic swaps); classification uses a verbose-correct guard plus a multi-layer slop detector (keyword, substring, numeric proximity, embedding similarity, token overlap) with structured-output schema parsing and confidence normalization. Answers are objectively verifiable.

2_construct_validity.justification: Explicitly controls confounds: Tier-1 prompts are scaffold-equalized to Tier-2+ (identical abstention/tool/calibration framing, differing only by presence of world rules) to remove the scaffolding confound, isolating counterfactual-override/inhibition. Confidence elicitation feeds SDT meta-d' so it measures metacognitive monitoring rather than raw knowledge.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, sectioned, well-commented notebook; uses kbench, parallelized inference with caching, structured-output dataclass schema, protobuf sanitization, robust _safe_call exception wrapping, dynamic-threshold SDT with NaN/inf guards, and tool execution with confidence-cost capping. Embedding model load is wrapped in try/except fallback.

4_sample_size.justification: Generates N=600 problems (150 per tier x 4 tiers, balanced across 4 problem types) and asserts >=500 valid trials for stable SDT estimates; vastly exceeds the 20-item threshold and enables per-tier/per-type analysis.

5_metric_design.justification: Computes many genuinely distinct dimensions: d' and meta-d' (SDT efficiency), ECE/calibration bins, asymmetric Brier ACE, selective-prediction score (+1/0/-5), tool-utility, four failure-mode rates, inverse-scaling index, plus bootstrap 95% CIs. Markdown justifies the metrics; calibration is explicitly elicited and scored.

6_problem_statement.justification: Header markdown clearly names the track (Metacognition), tasks, primary metric (meta-d'/d'), failure modes, and dataset; it states what is measured but does not deeply motivate why or what gap it fills relative to prior metacognition benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Section-by-section markdown headers (World-Swap engine, slop detection, dataset generator, evaluation, prompts, aggregator), inline docstrings for every metric, and printed dataset breakdown/diagnostic profile provide a reproducible walkthrough of data generation, scoring, and metric interpretation.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a specific sub-capability (metacognitive monitoring / inhibitory control under counterfactual rules) with explicit experimental controls: scaffold-equalized Tier1 baseline vs Tier2+ swaps, a difficulty gradient (tiers 1-4), and an inverse-scaling index, allowing performance deltas to be attributed to the target mechanism.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are organized into a 4x4 taxonomy (4 difficulty tiers x 4 problem types) and the aggregator reports per-tier accuracy/confidence and per-failure-mode rates, revealing where models fail (e.g., Tier1Acc 0.85 vs Tier4Acc 0.65) rather than just an overall score.

10_model_coverage.justification: The captured run shows a single model (claude-opus-4-7) via kbench.llm; no cross-model comparison results are present in the notebook.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth exists in the dataset (deterministic as-of answers), but verification is broken or fragile across notebooks: the eval notebook scores free-form LLM output against internal encoded IDs (e.g. 'slow:spec_15:d14:v5') via substring/token overlap, yielding 0/734; the v2/v4 notebooks never call the LLM and merely assert the Python store returns non-None (and one uses a nonexistent 'expected_value' field). Only task-2 has a sensible (but still substring-based, 'v2' in 'v26') check.

2_construct_validity.justification: Several 'tasks' fail to measure the model at all: temporalbenchv4 and temporalbench-v2-pccc pass llm as an unused parameter and instead test the authors' TemporalAttentionStore retrieval logic, so reported scores (942/942, 734/734) reflect the data structure, not the LLM. The eval notebook's encoded-ID answer format guarantees failures unrelated to temporal reasoning. Clear mismatch between claim and what is measured.

3_code_quality.justification: Code runs and is moderately organized with helper functions, but has serious defects: tasks that ignore the llm argument, computed-but-unused 'answer'/'expected' variables, bare 'except Exception: pass' swallowing all errors in the eval loop, hardcoded placeholder 'key_findings', and a staleness notebook built entirely on mock hardcoded data.

4_sample_size.justification: The dataset is large (v1 has 2000 questions; per-task families number in the hundreds, e.g. 734 OverlapQuery, 942 CurrentQuery; task-2 evaluates 2000 rows), well above 20 items even where scoring is flawed.

5_metric_design.justification: Effectively a single accuracy/pass-rate metric per task with no calibration, latency, or secondary dimension; the richer 'System A/B', TRS, and correlation findings are hardcoded strings in the output JSON, not actually computed. Several metrics are meaningless because the LLM is bypassed.

6_problem_statement.justification: The eval notebook header names the capability (temporal reasoning: validity windows vs decay) and track and gives basic context, but does not substantiate the motivation or gap, and the strongest framing claims (decay vs validity windows) are not actually evaluated in code.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Dataset has a metadata.json listing all version/seed resources and there is brief notebook markdown and a data-structure description, but scoring rules, the answer encoding scheme, and the relationship between the store and the LLM evaluation are undocumented and inconsistent across the five notebooks.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a clear domain (point-in-time temporal fact retrieval, with adversarial PastQueryTrap/Reversion/Overlap families serving as intended controls), but the execution does not isolate the capability: the kept runs either bypass the model or fail on formatting, so failing/passing reveals little about model temporal reasoning.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are organized into a structured taxonomy of task families (AsOfQA, ChangeDetection, CausalQuery, PastQueryTrap, CurrentQuery, DecayTrap, OverlapQuery, Reversion) across versions v1-v4 and seeds 0-2 plus an adversarial set, and tasks are reported per family, providing strong potential for a performance gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each notebook runs a single injected kbench.llm (visible runs include gemini-2.5-flash, glm-5, claude-opus-4-7 but each in its own separate notebook with one model); no within-task cross-model comparison is performed.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Verification is fully deterministic: code-golf runs the model's submitted code plus pre-validated hidden assert tests in a fresh namespace; regex-golf checks the regex fully matches the MATCH list and rejects the DO_NOT_MATCH list; EGAT computes exact embedding-geometry distances. Pass/fail and the continuous score are computed from objective execution, not judgment.

2_construct_validity.justification: Each task isolates a well-defined capability (constraint-satisfying code/regex compression; navigating a third-party embedding space) and the metric (fractional reduction toward a verified baseline) directly reflects it. Hidden tests prevent gaming, and the multi-trial reprompt loop with feedback isolates optimization-under-constraint rather than one-shot luck.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular, heavily commented notebooks using kbench @task, regex-based option extraction, sandboxed exec with truncated tracebacks, a 10-trial feedback loop, and notably robust handling of system-prompt rejection (detect-once, restart task with baked-in prompt). EGAT wraps judge-model loading and parsing/validation carefully.

4_sample_size.justification: Each notebook contains only 5 evaluation items (5 golf tasks / 5 regex sessions / 5 EGAT prompts), below the 10-item threshold; the multi-trial reprompting increases attempts per item but not the number of distinct evaluation items.

5_metric_design.justification: Uses a continuous, well-justified objective metric (fractional character/distance reduction vs a verified baseline, with -1.0 penalty for no valid solution, aggregated as mean across tasks) rather than plain accuracy; combines a validity dimension (passes hidden tests) with an efficiency/compression dimension, and the scoring rationale is documented in markdown.

6_problem_statement.justification: Each notebook's markdown names the capability and scoring clearly (e.g., 'evaluate syntactical efficiency and algorithmic compression', 'pattern compression and constraint reasoning'), but does not connect to a broader research question or articulate the gap relative to existing benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Strong notebook documentation: markdown overview tables of all tasks with baseline sizes, explicit scoring formulas, configuration-flag tables (EGAT), and inline comments explaining the reprompt loop, baseline pre-validation, and edge-case handling, sufficient to reproduce the evaluation.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes specific sub-capabilities (constraint-bounded compression / embedding-space navigation) with a clear hypothesis (shorter-yet-valid solutions indicate stronger constraint reasoning) and a difficulty gradient across the 5 tasks of increasing baseline size/constraint complexity, allowing graded performance attribution.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly organized with labeled, increasing-difficulty tasks/sessions (baseline char counts 449..1465 for code, regex lengths 21..56 with named constraint types), and per-task scores are reported separately, yielding a clear performance gradient across the structured set.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each task is run against a single injected kbench.llm; the visible outputs show one model per notebook (EGAT's all-MiniLM is the judge embedding model, not an evaluated LLM), with no cross-model comparison.
